claims.claim_text: In March 2018 in Tempe, Arizona, an Uber developmental automated driving system struck and killed a pedestrian walking a bicycle, and the NTSB found the system never accurately classified her as a pedestrian or predicted her path in time.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.rationale: The official NTSB investigation page directly supports the crash date, location, pedestrian-with-bicycle context, and the perception/path-prediction failure, but ntsb.gov is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The module attributes the Tempe failure specifically to a camera-feed CNN, which overstates what the NTSB source directly says.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: The Convolutional Neural Network processing the camera feeds struggled to classify the object in the darkness, oscillating wildly between predicting a "vehicle," a "bicycle," and an "other." This indecision delayed the critical braking response by precious seconds.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The vehicle's perception system repeatedly changed how it classified the pedestrian and did not predict her path accurately enough to trigger a timely braking response.
claims.rationale: The NTSB documents a broader ADS perception-system failure, not the narrower CNN-on-camera-feeds attribution used here.

claims.claim_text: The paragraph claims the crash immediately halted autonomous testing nationwide, erased billions in projected market capitalization, and fundamentally changed AI regulation.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: The incident immediately halted autonomous testing programs nationwide, erasing billions of dollars in projected market capitalization across the autonomous driving sector and fundamentally altering the regulatory landscape for AI deployment.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The crash intensified scrutiny of autonomous-vehicle safety and became a major reference point in later debates about testing and oversight.
claims.rationale: This bundles several sweeping economic and policy claims that are not supportable from a single authoritative source as written.

claims.claim_text: Hubel and Wiesel's 1959 cat visual-cortex experiments reported neurons responding selectively to features such as oriented stimuli.
claims.span_hint: section: The Computer Vision Revolution: Biological Roots, Did You Know
claims.rationale: The underlying experiment is real and directly documented in the original paper, but PMC is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Hubel and Wiesel received the 1981 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for discoveries about information processing in the visual system.
claims.span_hint: section: The Computer Vision Revolution: Biological Roots, Did You Know
claims.rationale: The Nobel site directly supports the award year and motivation, but nobelprize.org is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Hubel and Wiesel's follow-on visual-cortex work distinguished simple and complex receptive-field behavior, including spatial invariance properties associated with complex cells.
claims.span_hint: section: The Computer Vision Revolution: Biological Roots, numbered list after Did You Know
claims.rationale: The classic 1962 paper is the right primary source for the simple/complex-cell characterization, but PMC is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Dilated convolutions expand receptive field while preserving resolution, which is useful for dense prediction tasks such as semantic segmentation.
claims.rationale: The paper's abstract directly states that dilated convolutions aggregate context without losing resolution for dense prediction.

claims.claim_text: LeNet-5 was introduced in 1998 by Yann LeCun and became an early successful CNN for document and check-recognition tasks.
claims.rationale: LeCun's canonical LeNet page is the obvious primary source for this historical claim, but the domain is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: AlexNet's 2012 ImageNet result cut top-5 error to 15.3%, far below the prior runner-up result reported in the same paper.
claims.span_hint: section: AlexNet (2012): The Deep Learning Big Bang, Did You Know
claims.rationale: The NeurIPS paper directly reports the winning 15.3% top-5 error and comparison to the previous best, but papers.neurips.cc is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The clause about AlexNet fundamentally shifting the entire technology industry is too sweeping to support as written.
claims.span_hint: section: AlexNet (2012): The Deep Learning Big Bang, Did You Know
claims.anchor_text: fundamentally shifting the entire technology industry toward deep learning.
claims.suggested_rewrite: helping make deep convolutional networks central to modern computer vision.
claims.rationale: The historical impact is real, but this industry-wide causal claim is broader than the cited benchmark result can honestly bear.

claims.claim_text: The VGG paper evaluated very deep CNNs built from very small 3x3 convolution filters.
claims.span_hint: section: VGGNet (2014): The Power of Simplicity, Did You Know
claims.rationale: The VGG abstract explicitly says the architecture uses very small 3x3 convolution filters.

claims.span_hint: section: VGGNet (2014): The Power of Simplicity, Did You Know
claims.rationale: TorchVision's VGG16 weights metadata records 138,357,544 parameters, which supports the module's rounded figure.

claims.claim_text: The Inception/GoogLeNet architecture runs multiple convolutional paths at different scales and concatenates their outputs.
claims.span_hint: section: GoogLeNet/Inception (2014): Going Wider, Not Just Deeper
claims.rationale: The paper introduces the Inception architecture and describes its multi-branch design directly.

claims.claim_text: The ResNet sidebar combines an unstable citation count with a broad optimization claim in one sentence.
claims.span_hint: section: ResNet (2015): The Depth Breakthrough, Did You Know
claims.anchor_text: > **Did You Know?** Kaiming He's ResNet paper from 2015 is one of the most cited academic papers in modern history, surpassing 100,000 citations and enabling networks to exceed one hundred layers without vanishing gradients.
claims.suggested_rewrite: > **Did You Know?** Kaiming He's ResNet paper from 2015 became one of the field's defining papers and showed that skip connections make much deeper networks easier to optimize.
claims.rationale: The exact citation count is unstable and off-source, so the sentence should keep the architectural lesson and drop the floating number.

claims.claim_text: ResNet introduces residual learning with skip connections to make substantially deeper networks easier to optimize.
claims.span_hint: section: ResNet (2015): The Depth Breakthrough, paragraph after Did You Know
claims.rationale: The ResNet abstract directly states that residual learning eases optimization of substantially deeper networks.

claims.claim_text: EfficientNet formalizes compound scaling across depth, width, and resolution.
claims.rationale: The EfficientNet abstract explicitly says the method uniformly scales depth, width, and resolution with a compound coefficient.

claims.claim_text: The transfer-learning section overstates the need to mirror the exact pretraining augmentation pipeline.
claims.span_hint: section: Transfer Learning, sentence before normalization code
claims.anchor_text: For models utilizing these weights, you must mirror the exact data augmentation and preprocessing pipeline they were trained upon:
claims.suggested_rewrite: For pretrained weights, you should match the expected input sizing and normalization conventions, and keep your training and validation preprocessing internally consistent:
claims.rationale: Weight packages expose expected input transforms, but the exact original augmentation recipe is not generally a strict requirement in the way this sentence claims.

claims.claim_text: PyTorch AMP uses autocasting and gradient scaling to enable mixed-precision training that often lowers memory pressure.
claims.span_hint: section: Memory, Performance, and Common Mistakes, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: The official PyTorch AMP docs are the right primary source for autocast and GradScaler behavior, but docs.pytorch.org is off-allowlist.

further_reading.title: Very Deep Convolutional Networks for Large-Scale Image Recognition
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary VGG reference for the small-filter design pattern that shaped later CNNs.

further_reading.title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary ResNet paper for skip connections and deep-network optimization.

further_reading.title: EfficientNet: Rethinking Model Scaling for Convolutional Neural Networks
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for compound scaling across depth, width, and resolution.

notes: The core prose is about CNNs, even though the file name/title and generated appendix content drift back toward RNNs. I excluded code blocks, Mermaid diagrams, quiz/exercise material, and I did not emit rewrite claims for the duplicated resolution benchmark table because those rows appear twice verbatim (Markdown table plus fenced text), which makes deterministic substring-swap rewrites unsafe without a source-backed cleanup.
